thread.title: Leon County says goodbye to retiring Judge Gus Aikens, renames courthouse in his honor
thread.title_full: Leon County says goodbye to retiring Judge Gus Aikens, renames courthouse in his honor

author: , Tallahassee Democrat
title: Leon County says goodbye to retiring Judge Gus Aikens, renames courthouse in his honor
text: Leon County said goodbye to its longest serving judge, Augustus "Gus" Aikens Jr., honoring his legacy in a special way. As of Friday, the courthouse across the street from the Florida Capitol is called the Judge Augustus D. Aikens Jr. Leon County Courthouse. Aikens served as a county judge for 27 years before being required to retire. The judicial retirement age was changed by voters in 2018 to 75 from 70. His last day was Monday. The county celebrated by throwing Aikens a memorable retirement party in the very courtroom in which he finished his career before the unveiling of the courthouse's new name. Friends and family spoke highly of the now-retired judge, reminiscing on the person he was and still is. Roger Messer, Aikens' former roommate at Florida State University, said he asked Aikens to be best man at his wedding. "Fifty-four years in March, I'll be married to the same pretty nurse, and Gus Aikens is still the best man I know," Messer said. More than 300 people attended the retirement ceremony, filling three courtrooms with overflow seating — a testament to the respect he has earned and the lives he's touched along the way. The event was filled with much laughter, smiles and standing ovations applauding the long-serving judge's years of service. Michael Beckham, a former fraternity brother at North Florida College, said he knew Aikens was "destined for greater things." Telling the story of the two times he's ever stood before a judge, Beckham said, "If I ever had to stand before a judge again, I would want it to be Augustus Aikens." Aikens was first appointed to the bench by then-Gov. Lawton Chiles in 1997, then elected in 2000, 2006, 2012 and 2018. Prior to his judgeship, Aikens operated his own practice, and worked for the Florida Division of Administrative Services. Aikens also served as a reservist in the army for 30 years. "It's been a joy working with each of you," Aikens said to the judges and courthouse employees that surrounded him. "In fact, that's one of the things I'll miss the most." Aikens choked back tears during his closing remarks. "It's been a journey that can only be described as a labor of love," he said. "Today belongs to God's plan, not mine." Who's next? Voters elected LaShawn Riggans to take Aikens' place on the bench. The newest judge narrowly won the seat in the general election earlier this month after being pushed into a runoff with Robert Churchill. They were the top two vote-getters in the August primary, having knocked Cydnee Brown out of the running. In the general election, Riggans ended up with 50.48% and Churchill had 49.52% — only 67 votes apart at one point in the night. Riggans, 56, is ‘of counsel’ at the Nabors, Giblin & Nickerson law firm, also in Tallahassee, and has been Deputy County Attorney for Leon County. (Of counsel refers to attorneys who work on a case-to-case basis at a firm and not as an associate or partner.) In Florida, county judges hear misdemeanors, small claims cases, traffic infractions and landlord-tenant disputes, among other matters. Riggans' first day is Tuesday, Jan. 7, 2025. Breaking & trending news reporter Elena Barrera can be reached at [ebarrera@tallahassee.com](mailto:ebarrera@tallahassee.com). Follow her on X: @elenabarreraaa .
